items.title: Train a Personal Model From iMessage
items.takeaway: Use GPT-5.6 to build and run a local training pipeline that learns a personal writing style from private message history.
items.body_notes: The creator reports that one prompt produced the full training pipeline, trained a model locally on a Mac from iMessage history, and generated replies in the creator's style.
items.decision_reason: Concrete end-to-end local training workflow with a stated data source, environment, and output.
items.prompt_boundary: No exact reusable prompt text is present in the supplied JSON; omit the prompt block.

items.title: Run a Week-Long Voxel Manhattan Build
items.takeaway: Give a long-running coding agent a voxel Manhattan build and let it work autonomously over multiple days.
items.body_notes: The creator says GPT-5.6 Sol produced a detailed voxel Manhattan in a single autonomous run that lasted almost a week.
items.decision_reason: Specific long-horizon build with a named output and an explicit run duration.

items.title: Turn a Spoken Spec Into a Service
items.takeaway: Turn a spoken natural-language specification into an end-to-end service build.
items.body_notes: OpenAI Developers highlights Ramp engineer Sam Kronick using GPT-5.6 to build an entire service from a natural-language specification.
items.decision_reason: Named practitioner, clear input boundary, and a concrete end-to-end software output.

items.title: Build a Personal Business Operating System
items.takeaway: Start with one repetitive task, grant Codex access to the relevant tools, and expand the workflow after it works reliably.
items.body_notes: The 49-minute masterclass describes inbox cards with drafted replies, a unified Slack and meeting feed, an agent email address, learned repeatable skills, and goals that run for up to 20 hours.
items.decision_reason: Detailed multi-step workflow, concrete integrations, operating advice, and a full tutorial source.
items.prompt_boundary: No exact reusable prompt text is present in the supplied JSON; omit the prompt block.

items.title: Add an Autonomous Critique Pass
items.takeaway: Use a second agent as a critique pass when reviewing GPT-5.6's own work.
items.body_notes: The creator reports that GPT-5.6 autonomously started another agent to critique its work even though no skill or instruction explicitly requested that behavior.
items.decision_reason: Concrete observed agent behavior with an explicit absence of preconfigured critique instructions.

items.title: Edit a Hype Video From One Instruction
items.takeaway: Drop an MP4 into the workspace and request a concise promotional cut in natural language.
items.body_notes: The creator demonstrates the workflow from an MP4 to a 60-second hype video and links a full YouTube tutorial.
items.decision_reason: Clear input, instruction, output duration, and linked tutorial.

items.title: Produce Brand-Aware Ads Through MCP
items.takeaway: Connect GPT-5.6 to an ad-production MCP so the agent can use brand context, video tools, and reusable skills in one workflow.
items.body_notes: Creatify says its MCP lets an agent move from an idea to a finished ad in minutes with access to the full ad stack.
items.decision_reason: Named integration with an explicit tool boundary, retained brand context, and a concrete finished-ad output.

items.title: Extend Existing Designs in Figma Make
items.takeaway: Use GPT-5.6 in Figma Make when building from an existing design, then compare output strength and token efficiency.
items.body_notes: Figma reports early tests with stronger outputs from existing designs and improved token efficiency.
items.decision_reason: First-party product integration with a specific existing-design workflow and stated test observations.

items.title: Compare Intelligence, Coding, and Cost
items.takeaway: Use third-party benchmark results to choose Sol, Terra, or Luna by intelligence, coding performance, and cost per task.
items.body_notes: Artificial Analysis reports Sol at 59 on its Intelligence Index and 80 on its Coding Agent Index; Terra and Luna score 55 and 51 on intelligence at lower cost per task. These are third-party benchmark claims from the source.
items.decision_reason: Independent pre-release evaluation with named indexes, model tiers, scores, and cost-per-task comparisons.
items.prompt_boundary: No exact reusable prompt text is present in the supplied JSON; omit the prompt block.

items.title: Test Physics Quality Against Cost
items.takeaway: Benchmark visual polish and physical correctness separately before choosing Sol Ultra for browser-based simulations.
items.body_notes: Atomic Chat compared four models on three HTML5 canvas physics scenes. It reports Sol Ultra used 32.9K tokens at $0.33 versus GPT-5.5 at 12.4K tokens and $0.11, with weaker physics despite greater visual detail.
items.decision_reason: Reproducible comparison setup with shared task prompts, token counts, costs, and a stated negative result.
